content: The square of resultant of two equal electric field vectors is three times their product. Angle between them is
explanation:
Let each field have magnitude $E$. Then the resultant $R$ of two vectors at angle $\theta$ satisfies

$$ |R|^2 \;=\; E^2 + E^2 + 2E^2\cos\theta \;=\; 2E^2(1+\cos\theta). $$

We’re told $|R|^2 = 3\,(E\cdot E)=3E^2$. So

$$ 2E^2(1+\cos\theta)=3E^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 1+\cos\theta=\tfrac32 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \cos\theta=\tfrac12 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \theta=\frac\pi3. $$

Answer: Option D, $\displaystyle \frac\pi3$.

content: If $$\alpha, \beta$$ and $$\gamma$$ are the angles between the vectors $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{Q}}$$, and $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{R}}$$ and $$\alpha=90^{\circ}$$ as shown in figure. the product of $$(\vec{Q} \times \vec{R}) \cdot \vec{Q}$$ is equal to

The sides of a parallelogram are represented by vectors $$\vec{p}=5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$ and $$\vec{q}=3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$. Then, the area of the parallelogram is
explanation:
Area of a parallelogram $$=|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}|$$ where, a and b are sides of parallelogram.

Given, $$\vec{a} = \vec{p} = 5\widehat i - 4\widehat j + 3\widehat k$$

and $$\vec{b} = \vec{q} = 3\widehat i + 2\widehat j - \widehat k$$

$$\therefore \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \left| {\matrix{ {\widehat i} & {\widehat j} & {\widehat k} \cr 5 & { - 4} & 3 \cr 3 & 2 & { - 1} \cr } } \right|$$

$$\Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \widehat i(4 - 6) - \widehat j( - 5 - 9) + \widehat k(10 + 12)$$

$$\Rightarrow \vec{a} \times \vec{b} = - 2\widehat i + 14\widehat j + 22\widehat k$$

Thus, $$|\vec{a} \times \vec{b}| = \sqrt {{{(2)}^2} + {{(14)}^2} + {{(22)}^2}} = \sqrt {684} $$ sq units

content: The resultant of two forces acting at an angle of 120$$^\circ$$ is 10 kg-W and is perpendicular to one of the forces. That force is
explanation:
From the diagram we get,

$$\tan 30^\circ = {1 \over {\sqrt 3 }} = {x \over {10}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow x = {{10} \over {\sqrt 3 }}$$

content: A particle starts moving from point (2, 10, 1). Displacement for the particle is $$8\widehat i - 2\widehat j + \widehat k$$. The final coordinates of the particle is
explanation:
Position vector of starting point $$(2,10,1)$$ of particle is given as

$$\mathbf{r}_1=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+10 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

If final co-ordinate of the particle be $$(x, y, z)$$, then its position vector

$$\mathbf{r}_2=x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+z \hat{\mathbf{k}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Displacement, $$\Delta \mathbf{r}=\mathbf{r}_2-\mathbf{r}_1$$

$$\begin{aligned} & 8 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}=x \hat{\mathbf{i}}+y \hat{\mathbf{j}}+z \hat{\mathbf{k}}-(2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+10 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 8 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}=(x-2) \hat{\mathbf{i}}+(y-10) \hat{\mathbf{j}}+(z-1) \hat{\mathbf{k}} \end{aligned}$$

Comparing the coefficients of $$\hat{\mathbf{i}}, \hat{\mathbf{j}}$$ and $$\hat{\mathbf{k}}$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned}\quad x-2=8, y-10 & =-2, z-1=1 \\ \Rightarrow \quad x=10, y & =8, z=2 \\ \therefore \text { Final co-ordinate } & =(10,8,2) \end{aligned}$$
